There are no behavioral differences, logic changes, or scope expansions between the two provided excerpts; they represent the same clinical instruction. The only distinction is the metadata label (Taxonomy description vs. Feature) and the source identifier, which does not affect the clinical coding logic.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign code U07.1, COVID-19, as the principal/first-listed diagnosis when the reason for the encounter is a nonrespiratory manifestation. -> icd (New Version): Assign code U07.1, COVID-19, as the principal/first-listed diagnosis when the reason for the encounter is a nonrespiratory manifestation.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign code(s) for the specific manifestation(s) as additional diagnoses. -> icd (New Version): Assign code(s) for the specific manifestation(s) as additional diagnoses.

findings.summary: The older version establishes an exception allowing code U07.1 for active confirmed COVID-19 diagnoses based solely on provider documentation, overriding standard inpatient guidelines. The newer version introduces a separate requirement to assign code Z86.16 specifically for patients with a documented history of COVID-19. These are not contradictory updates to the same rule but rather the introduction of a new feature for a different clinical state (history vs. active), resulting in weak overlap rather than a direct guideline revision.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Assign code U07.1 for active confirmed COVID-19 diagnoses based on provider documentation without requiring a positive test result. -> icd (New Version): Assign code Z86.16 for patients with a documented history of COVID-19.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Prohibits using U07.1 for 'suspected' or 'possible' cases, requiring symptom coding instead. -> icd (New Version): Requires explicit documentation confirming the patient has a history of COVID-19 to use Z86.16.

findings.summary: The older version and the new version contain identical behavioral instructions, requirements, and raw text excerpts regarding the designation of bone sites over joint sites. There are no functional changes, clarifications, or reversals in the logic between the two versions; the text is effectively a duplicate. The only distinction is the metadata label 'Administrative Update' reflecting the versioning process rather than a change in clinical coding logic.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): For conditions affecting the upper or lower end of a bone (e.g., avascular necrosis, osteoporosis), the site designation must be the bone itself. -> icd (New Version): For conditions affecting the upper or lower end of a bone (e.g., avascular necrosis, osteoporosis), the site designation must be the bone itself.

findings.summary: The older version explicitly detailed the logic for handling multiple sites (using 'multiple sites' codes or multiple codes) and required laterality for all applicable codes. The newer version retains the core requirement for site and laterality designations but omits the specific procedural instructions for managing multiple sites, likely due to a formatting or extraction limitation rather than a clinical logic change. The fundamental rule that codes must represent the specific bone, joint, or muscle remains consistent across both versions.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Codes within Chapter 13 must include site and laterality designations; for conditions where multiple sites are usually involved, a 'multiple sites' code should be used if available, otherwise multiple codes must be used. -> icd (New Version): Codes within Chapter 13 must include site and laterality designations; the specific instruction regarding the selection of 'multiple sites' codes versus multiple codes for multi-site conditions is omitted.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Laterality must be explicitly designated for all applicable codes within Chapter 13. -> icd (New Version): Laterality must be explicitly designated for all applicable codes in this chapter.

findings.summary: The older version and the new version present the exact same clinical logic for assigning codes under Section I.C.21, focusing on factors influencing health status and contact with health services. Both versions require explicit documentation linking the aftercare need to a specific health status factor and mandate that the primary condition be established. There are no substantive changes to the coding rules, definitions, or restrictions between the two texts; the differences are purely in the phrasing of the taxonomy descriptions and extracted profiles.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Factors influencing health status and contact with health services are categorized under Aftercare | Codes in this section describe the need for ongoing care or contact with health services following a primary condition | These factors influence health status and are used to capture the continuity of care requirements -> icd (New Version): Factors influencing health status and contact with health services are categorized under Section I.C.21 | Aftercare codes are used to document ongoing treatment and monitoring following an initial encounter | These factors influence health status and the frequency of contact with health services
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Documentation must explicitly link the aftercare need to a specific health status factor or contact requirement | The primary condition must be established to justify the aftercare classification -> icd (New Version): Documentation must clearly indicate the aftercare nature of the service provided | The encounter must be for ongoing treatment or monitoring related to a previous condition or procedure

findings.summary: The older version explicitly instructed coders to query the provider for conflicting documentation, whereas the newer version clarifies that assignment should be based on medical record documentation from other clinicians if the provider's documentation is missing. Both versions maintain the strict prohibition against assigning complication codes from Chapter 19 for BIA-ALCL and enforce the same rules for assigning bilateral versus unilateral codes based on the existence of the condition across encounters.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Query the patient's provider for clarification if there is conflicting medical record documentation regarding the affected side. -> icd (New Version): Base code assignment on medical record documentation from other clinicians if laterality is not documented by the patient's provider.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Explicit instruction to query the patient's provider for clarification when medical record documentation regarding the affected side is conflicting. -> icd (New Version): Explicit instruction to assign the bilateral code for the first encounter treating a bilateral condition, including for the encounter to treat the first side.

findings.summary: The older version (FY25 Guidelines) and the new version (icd) present the exact same text for the 'Code Title Note Definitions' feature, indicating no change in the clinical logic or rule definition. The behavior remains consistent: notes appear immediately under three-character titles to define category content or provide examples. findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): This note appears immediately under a three-character code title to further define, or give examples of, the content of the category. -> icd (New Version): This note appears immediately under a three-character code title to further define, or give examples of, the content of the category.

findings.summary: The two versions present the exact same clinical rules regarding the assignment of R40.20 and the prohibition of unspecified codes for medically induced or sedated patients. The text excerpts are identical, and the behavioral lists match perfectly, indicating no change in clinical logic or code assignment rules. The only differences are minor formatting variations in the 'New items' and 'Deprecated' sections, which reflect an administrative update rather than a guideline revision.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Explicit prohibition against coding unspecified coma for medically induced or sedated patients. -> icd (New Version): Explicit prohibition against coding unspecified coma for sedated or medically induced states.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Requirement to withhold Glasgow coma scale reporting for medically induced or sedated patients. -> icd (New Version): No specific new item listed for this rule in the 'New items' section, though the behavior remains consistent.

findings.summary: The older version (FY25 Guidelines) and the new version (icd) present identical logic for interpreting 'see' and 'see also' instructions in the Alphabetic Index. Both explicitly state that 'see' requires navigation to the referenced term while 'see also' is optional if the primary term suffices. The only differences are minor phrasing variations in the extracted profiles (e.g., 'useful entries' vs 'additional entries') rather than substantive changes to the coding rules or clinical logic. Consequently, no functional delta exists between the two versions regarding this specific feature.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): 'see also' indicates an additional main term that may provide useful entries but is not mandatory. -> icd (New Version): 'see also' indicates an additional main term that may provide useful entries but is not mandatory.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): 'see' instruction indicates another term must be referenced to locate the correct code. -> icd (New Version): 'see' instruction indicates another term must be referenced to locate the correct code.

findings.summary: The older version explicitly states that clinicians other than the patient's provider must document the hazards, while the new version reiterates this requirement with slightly different phrasing regarding 'non-provider clinicians'. Both versions mandate the use of Z55-Z65 codes and require the hazards to be linked to socioeconomic or psychosocial circumstances. There is no substantive change in the clinical logic, code assignment rules, or the specific actors required to perform the documentation; the text represents a restatement of the same administrative guideline.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Clinicians other than the patient's provider must document psychosocial hazards related to socioeconomic circumstances. -> icd (New Version): Documentation by clinicians other than the patient's provider is required for hazards related to socioeconomic and psychosocial circumstances.

findings.summary: The older version (Chunk 408) and the new version (Chunk 841) contain the exact same text for the 'Medical Record Specificity Coding' feature, including the instruction to code to the highest level of specificity supported by documentation. There are no behavioral differences, rule changes, or scope expansions between these two specific excerpts; they represent a static guideline instruction repeated across versions. Consequently, the change type is classified as an Administrative Update because the content remains functionally identical despite the version update.
findings.detail: ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Code to the highest level of specificity when supported by the medical record documentation. -> icd (New Version): Code to the highest level of specificity when supported by the medical record documentation.

findings.section: Factors Influencing Health Status and Contact
findings.summary: The older version provided general instructions on using follow-up codes for monitoring, while the newer version refines the requirement to explicitly distinguish between routine monitoring and active intervention. Both versions maintain the rule against using these codes as primary diagnoses when a specific disease exists, but the new version adds a specific workflow element to the taxonomy description regarding tracking patient progress. The key behavioral difference lies in the precision required for documentation to support the distinction between passive monitoring and active treatment outcomes.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Enhanced specificity in coding for post-treatment monitoring and long-term health status factors. -> icd (New Version): Enhanced specificity required for follow-up codes to distinguish between routine monitoring and active intervention.
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Follow-up codes are used to indicate that a patient is being monitored for a condition or the effects of treatment. -> icd (New Version): Follow-up codes are used to indicate that a patient is being monitored for a condition or treatment outcome.

findings.summary: The core clinical logic regarding the classification of prophylactic organ removal as a factor influencing health status remains identical in both versions. The only differences are minor phrasing adjustments in the 'New items' section, specifically changing 'where applicable' to 'if applicable' and adding 'Mandatory linkage,' which represent administrative refinements to the reporting requirements rather than changes to the underlying coding rules.
findings.detail:
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Inclusion of specific external cause codes for prophylactic procedures where applicable -> icd (New Version): Inclusion of specific external cause codes for prophylactic organ removal if applicable to the encounter
ICD-10-CM FY25 Guidelines October 1, 2024 (Older): Updated guidelines for linking prophylactic removal factors to specific health status codes in Section I.C. 21 -> icd (New Version): Mandatory linkage of the removal event to the specific health status factor code in Section I.C. 21
